conbody.p: Skulls and skeletons are prominent symbols of Halloween, commonly representing death, mortality, and the physical remains of the human body. Their stark visual form makes them powerful symbols that emphasize what lies beneath the surface of everyday life.

section.title: Historical and Cultural Associations
section.p: Human remains have been used symbolically across cultures to represent both remembrance and impermanence. Skulls, in particular, have appeared in art, religious objects, and memorial practices as reminders of mortality.
Over time, these associations were absorbed into Halloween imagery, where skeletons became familiar figures in folklore, storytelling, and later popular entertainment.

section.title: Symbolic Meaning
section.p: Symbolically, skulls and skeletons represent the inevitability of death and the fragility of life. They may also suggest equality, as all people share the same basic physical structure regardless of status or identity.
In Halloween contexts, skeleton imagery is often stylized or exaggerated to soften its impact, transforming a symbol of death into something playful or theatrical.

section.title: Modern Halloween Usage
section.p: In modern Halloween celebrations, skulls and skeletons are widely used in decorations, costumes, and themed displays. They may appear as realistic props or as cartoon-like figures designed to be humorous rather than frightening.
Skeleton imagery is also commonly used in seasonal media and merchandise due to its immediate recognizability and strong visual contrast.

example.title: Common Uses of Skulls and Skeletons During Halloween

ul.li: Decorative skeletons displayed indoors or outdoors
Skull motifs in costumes, masks, and accessories
Visual elements in haunted attractions and themed events
